Justification: The Methods explicitly define and compute a numerical “Resilience Index (RI = 1 − γ)” within an “exposure-sensitivity-resilience” framework and use it to calculate EVI via Equations (1)–(5), reporting RI and EVI results.
The Methods explicitly compute a resilience index (Rs = Gpost/Gprev) and related resistance/recovery indices from BAI before/during/after the 1985–1986 drought and report resilience results.

The study explicitly quantifies drought resilience using Lloret et al. (2011) resilience indices (Rt, Rc, Rs, RRs) with provided equations and calculated results for stems and roots across drought events and forest stages.

The study quantitatively computes an Ecosystem Resilience Index (ERI) within a Vigor-Organization-Resilience-Stress (VORS) ecosystem health framework using an explicit formula (ERI = Σ Ai×RCi) and reports ERI results.

The study explicitly frames demographic buffering as a mechanism of resilience and quantitatively computes variance buffering via the coefficient of variation of annual population growth rates (λt) and stochastic growth rate (λS) using stochastic matrix projection models and simulations.
The study explicitly quantifies ecosystem resilience (ER) within a VOR framework using the formula ER = Σ(Ai × RCi) and reports resilience results (e.g., Table 8).

Although resilience is discussed conceptually (e.g., “potential resilience effects” and “may be interpreted as structural resilience”), the study does not define or compute a quantitative resilience metric or explicitly state any calculated variable is a proxy for resilience.

The study explicitly quantifies ecological network resilience by defining and computing robustness and redundancy indices (Eqs. 16, 23) and then calculating resilience R via Eqs. 24–25 with reported resilience results (e.g., PRD 1990 = 0.364; BTH 2020 = 0.351).

The study explicitly defines resilience as a proxy (maximum biomass growth rate rmax), provides equations to estimate it (Ricker-based latent biomass model), and reports calculated resilience values (daily maximum percent increase in biomass 5–42%).

The paper explicitly states that functional evenness (FEve) is used as a proxy for community resilience and quantitatively computes FEve (via dbFD) and reports results comparing FEve among community types.
The study quantitatively computes ecological resilience using an explicit ER index (ERI) formula (Eq. 2) based on weighted EDF, ECC, and ERC and reports spatiotemporal ER results.

The study explicitly quantifies ecosystem resilience (and resistance) using ARx model coefficients (e.g., Eq. (1) where coefficient α is defined as resilience) and computes normalized resilience/resistance indices and a hydro-resistance index (Eq. (3)) with reported results.

The study explicitly quantifies ecological resilience via three measured dimensions (resistance, heterogeneity, recovery) and provides mathematical methods (logistic recovery model Eq. 1 and derived recovery time Eq. 2) to compute resilience-related results.

The study explicitly quantifies vegetation resilience using the posterior mean of negative lag-1 autocorrelation of MODIS EVI (estimated via a Bayesian dynamic linear model with equations provided) as a numerical proxy for resilience and reports resilience trends and changes.

Although resilience is mentioned conceptually, the study quantifies stem/biomass dynamics and LTRs but does not define, compute, or use any metric/index explicitly as resilience or a proxy for resilience.

Although “resilience” is mentioned conceptually (e.g., keywords and discussion), the study quantitatively analyzes plant/soil responses (height and Amax ratios, survival, nutrients, enzyme activities) without explicitly defining or computing any resilience metric or proxy as resilience.

Although “resilience” is discussed (e.g., “reduced resilience” and “overall resilience”), the study does not explicitly define or compute a quantitative resilience metric or proxy as resilience in its analyses.
